mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Taiwan on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the east. Taiwan is an island in the Pacific Ocean, about 180 km off the south-east coast of mainland China 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Taiwan?
mapQuestions.answer: Taipei, in the north of the island. Home to the famous Taipei 101 tower (once the world's tallest building, 2004-2010) and one of the best food cultures in Asia.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Taiwan speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Mandarin Chinese (the official language), Taiwanese Hokkien (spoken at home by many families), and Hakka. Some indigenous Austronesian languages are also still spoken in the mountains.

mapQuestions.question: Why is Taiwan a complicated political topic?
mapQuestions.answer: Taiwan governs itself as a democracy with its own president, parliament and military, but mainland China sees Taiwan as part of China. Taiwan has its own seat at the Olympics and many international bodies, often under different names.

mapQuestions.question: How mountainous is Taiwan?
mapQuestions.answer: Two-thirds of Taiwan is mountains. The island has over 200 peaks above 3,000 m, including Yushan (Jade Mountain) at 3,952 m, the tallest mountain in East Asia outside the Himalayas.

foods.name: Xiaolongbao
foods.description: Tiny soup-filled steamed dumplings, pinched into 18 folds at the top. Pick one up gently with chopsticks, nip a hole, slurp the broth out, then eat the wrapper.

foods.name: Beef noodle soup
foods.description: Taiwan's unofficial national dish: rich beef broth, big chunks of slow-braised beef, pickled mustard greens, and chewy hand-pulled noodles.

foods.name: Bubble tea
foods.description: Invented in Taichung, Taiwan in the 1980s: sweet milk tea with chewy black tapioca pearls at the bottom, drunk through a fat straw. Now sold in every city on Earth.

foods.name: Popcorn chicken
foods.description: Salt-and-pepper fried chicken pieces with crispy basil leaves on top. The Taiwan night-market classic, served in paper cones.

foods.name: Stinky tofu
foods.description: Fermented tofu, deep-fried until crisp on the outside and soft inside. Smells extraordinary (it really does), but tastes mild and savoury. Eaten with pickled cabbage.

foods.name: Pineapple cake
foods.description: Small buttery shortbread squares filled with sweet pineapple jam. The most popular Taiwanese souvenir, every Taipei airport shop sells boxes of them.

scavengerItems.label: A Taoist or Buddhist temple with red lanterns, incense smoke and dragon roofs
A night market with dozens of food stalls
The Taipei MRT (rated one of the cleanest subway systems in the world)
The Taipei 101 tower glowing at night
A misty mountain valley with hot springs (Taroko Gorge, Beitou)
Someone walking around with a giant bubble tea
A scooter (Taipei has more scooters than cars)
A Kenting or Green Island coral reef
The Alishan mountain railway (an old logging train winding through tea fields)
A 3,000-year-old cypress tree in Alishan National Forest
A "Pingxi" sky lantern released over the mountains

geoMatches.description: The capital, in the north. Modern subway, Taipei 101 tower, hot-spring district at Beitou, dozens of night markets, and Chiang Kai-shek Memorial Hall.
A spectacular marble canyon on the east coast, with cliffs rising sheer out of the river, ribbon waterfalls and old Japanese-era tunnels.
A high mountain area in the centre famous for its 3,000-year-old cypress trees, sunrise from above a sea of clouds, and Taiwan's best high-mountain tea.
The old capital, in the south. The original heart of Taiwanese culture and food, with the most temples per person of any Taiwanese city.
A national park at the southern tip of the island, with white-sand beaches, coral reefs, and the only proper tropical zone in Taiwan.
A large alpine lake in central Taiwan, shaped like a sun on one side and a crescent moon on the other. Surrounded by Thao indigenous villages and tea farms.

senses.smell: Smoky charcoal grilling at night markets, sweet steamed buns from a 7-Eleven, stinky tofu (you can't miss it), incense smoke at Taoist temples, fresh oolong tea in Alishan…
senses.hear: The Taipei MRT chime, "Ni hao!" and "Xie xie!", scooter horns at every junction, the hiss of dumpling baskets being lifted off bamboo steamers, drums at a temple festival…
senses.taste: Hot soup-filled xiaolongbao dumplings, rich beef-noodle soup, sweet milky bubble tea, smoky night-market chicken, fragrant high-mountain Alishan tea…
senses.feel: Humid summer heat, the cool mist of Taroko Gorge waterfalls, the steam of a Beitou hot spring on your back, the warmth of a soup dumpling in your fingers, the chewy bounce of tapioca pearls…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Who invented bubble tea?
stories.body: Bubble tea (also called "boba" or "pearl milk tea") was invented in Taichung, Taiwan, around 1986. Most accounts credit a young employee at Chun Shui Tang teahouse named Lin Hsiu Hui, who, during a staff meeting, dropped some sweet tapioca dessert into her iced milk tea on a whim. Everyone tasted it. It was great. The shop added it to the menu and it exploded across Taiwan, then Asia, then the world. Today bubble tea is a multi-billion-dollar global industry, with versions in nearly every major city. Lin and Chun Shui Tang are still going. You can visit the original teahouse in Taichung.

stories.question: Why are Taiwanese night markets so famous?
stories.body: A Taiwanese night market is an open-air street full of small food stalls, games and shops that opens around 5pm and runs until midnight. Some of the most famous in Taipei are Shilin, Raohe and Ningxia. Locals come for dinner, friends meet, kids drag their parents toward the games. Each stall specialises in just ONE thing done perfectly: scallion pancake here, popcorn chicken there, oyster omelette down the street. CNN, Time and Lonely Planet have all called Taiwanese night markets some of the best street food on Earth. Taipei has dozens. Smaller cities have one or two. Whole books have been written just trying to map them all.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Whole cities organise themselves around eating-outdoors-at-night. What food traditions does your home country share that come close?

stories.question: Why is high-mountain Taiwanese tea special?
stories.body: In the mountains of central Taiwan (Alishan, Lishan, Shan Lin Xi) grow some of the most prized teas in the world. The trees are oolong (partially fermented). The high altitude (1,000 to 2,400 m) means cool nights, slow-growing leaves, and concentrated flavours. Some farms still pluck leaves by hand. The very best Taiwanese high-mountain oolong sells for hundreds of dollars per kilo in Tokyo and London tea shops. Taiwan also invented the modern bubble tea industry. So Taiwan has the world's best ancient tea AND the most modern tea drink, all in one small island.

stories.question: Why is Taiwan complicated politically?
stories.body: In 1949, after a long civil war on mainland China, the losing side (the Republic of China, led by Chiang Kai-shek) retreated to Taiwan with around 2 million troops and civilians. They set up their government on the island and ruled as the "real" China for decades. The winning side (the People's Republic of China) controls the mainland. Today Taiwan is a fully democratic country with its own elected president, parliament, army, passport, currency, and one of Asia's most successful economies. But the People's Republic still claims Taiwan as part of China, and threatens force if Taiwan formally declares independence. The status quo (Taiwan governs itself, doesn't formally declare independence, China doesn't invade) has held for 75 years, and is one of the most carefully balanced political situations in the world.

stories.question: What is a sky-lantern festival?
stories.body: In the small mountain town of Pingxi, north-east of Taipei, locals release giant paper sky lanterns (heated by a small flame at the base) into the night sky on most weekends, but especially during the Lantern Festival (15th day of Lunar New Year). The lanterns float up by the hundreds, glowing like fireflies against the mountains. People write wishes on them in calligraphy ink first. The tradition is hundreds of years old: lanterns were originally used to send messages between villages during attacks, but became a symbol of peace and wishes for the future. Today it is one of Taiwan's most photographed and most beautiful traditions.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Taiwan so far?
Night market, temple, mountain, beach, which has felt the most "Taiwan"?
How many different night-market foods have you tried this trip?
Did you find a good bubble tea? What was your order?
Taiwan invented bubble tea AND grows some of the world's best old-school tea. How can one place do both?

wordSearch.hint: Taiwanese foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Houtong cat village cats
animals.description: A whole train-station village near Taipei (Houtong) has become a cat-lovers' paradise, with dozens of friendly cats lounging everywhere.

animals.name: Mikado pheasant
animals.description: A dark blue and shy mountain pheasant found only in Taiwan, on the back of the 1,000 NT$ bill.

animals.name: Tropical butterflies
animals.description: Taiwan has 400+ butterfly species. The Maolin Purple Butterfly Valley hosts the world's second-largest butterfly migration in winter.

animals.name: Formosan rock macaque
animals.description: A small native macaque living wild in Taiwan's mountain parks. Found only here.

animals.name: Black-faced spoonbill
animals.description: A long-billed wader that winters in big flocks on Taiwan's south-west coast wetlands. Globally endangered.

animals.name: Formosan black bear
animals.description: Taiwan's only native bear, with a distinctive white V on its chest. Endangered, lives in the mountains.

animals.name: Spinner dolphin
animals.description: Plays in pods off the east coast of Taiwan, especially Hualien.
